posts.title: What Is Nostr?
posts.description: A plain-language introduction to Nostr — the open protocol that lets you own your identity and communicate without relying on any single company or server.
posts.excerpt: Nostr is a simple, open protocol for decentralized communication. No company owns it. No server controls it. Here's how it works.
posts.seoTitle: What Is Nostr? A Beginner's Guide to the Open Protocol
posts.seoDescription: Learn what Nostr is, how it works, and why it matters. A plain-language introduction for professionals who want to understand decentralized communication.
posts.content:
Nostr stands for **Notes and Other Stuff Transmitted by Relays**. It is an open protocol — a set of rules — that lets people publish messages, build profiles, and communicate without depending on any single company.

No corporation runs Nostr. No one can shut it down. And no one can take away your account.

![When you post on Nostr, your note is broadcast to multiple independent relay servers simultaneously — no single server owns your content](/images/guides/what-is-nostr/broadcast-diagram.svg)

## How It Works

Every social platform you've used — LinkedIn, X, Instagram — works the same way: you create an account on their servers, and they control everything. Your posts, your followers, your data, your access. If the company goes down, changes its rules, or decides to ban you, there's nothing you can do.

Nostr works differently. It has two core concepts: **keys** and **relays**.

### Keys: Your Identity

When you join Nostr, you get a pair of cryptographic keys — think of them as a lock and a key.

- Your **public key** is your identity. It's like a username that belongs to you permanently. Anyone can see it.
- Your **private key** is your password. It proves you are who you say you are. Only you should ever have it.

These keys are not stored on anyone's server. They are generated on your device. No company issues them, and no company can revoke them. Your identity is yours.

<Callout type="tip">
Your public key starts with `npub` and your private key starts with `nsec`. Treat your private key like a bank password — never share it, never paste it into a website.
</Callout>

### Relays: The Network

Relays are simple servers that pass messages around. When you publish a post, your app sends it to one or more relays. When someone wants to read your posts, their app fetches them from those relays.

Here's the critical difference: **you are not locked into any single relay.** You can use one, five, or fifty. If a relay goes offline, your other relays still have your data. If a relay starts censoring content, you switch to a different one. No relay is "the Nostr server" — they are all interchangeable.

Think of it like email. Your email isn't trapped inside Gmail. You can use any provider, and they all talk to each other. Nostr works on a similar principle, but simpler and without needing to register anywhere.

![You choose which relays to use and can switch anytime — if one goes down, the others still have your posts](/images/guides/what-is-nostr/relay-nodes.svg)

## Why It Matters

The traditional internet is built on concentration. A handful of cloud providers (AWS, Azure, Cloudflare) host most of the services you use every day. When one of them fails, huge swaths of the internet go dark — as the world saw repeatedly in late 2025.

Nostr eliminates that single point of failure. There is no central server to go down, no corporate policy to change overnight, and no account to get suspended.

For professionals, this means:

- **Your content is portable.** Switch apps anytime — your posts and followers come with you.
- **Your identity is permanent.** No platform can delete your profile or lock you out.
- **Your network is resilient.** It works even when major infrastructure fails.

Nostr is not a company or a product. It is a protocol — like email or the web itself. Anyone can build on it. And that's exactly what makes it durable.

posts.title: Why Nostr Is Resilient (And the Internet Isn't)
posts.description: The 2025 cloud outages took down X, ChatGPT, LinkedIn, and public transit. Nostr's architecture is built to survive exactly this kind of failure.
posts.excerpt: AWS, Azure, and Cloudflare all failed in late 2025 — and took half the internet with them. Here's why Nostr doesn't have that problem.
posts.seoTitle: Why Nostr Is Resilient — And the Internet Isn't
posts.seoDescription: The 2025 cloud outages revealed how fragile the internet really is. Learn how Nostr's relay architecture avoids single points of failure by design.
posts.content:
In late 2025, three of the biggest cloud infrastructure providers — AWS, Azure, and Cloudflare — all experienced major outages. Not at the same time. But close enough to make a point.

When AWS went down, it didn't just affect Amazon. It took out streaming services, banking apps, and enterprise tools that millions of people rely on daily. When Azure failed, Microsoft's own products went dark — and so did the businesses built on top of them. When Cloudflare had issues, websites and APIs around the world became unreachable.

X went offline. ChatGPT went offline. LinkedIn went offline. Public transit systems in some cities stopped displaying real-time information. Not because of a cyberattack. Because a single provider had a bad day.

![When a major cloud provider fails, every service built on top of it goes down simultaneously — social media, AI tools, enterprise software, banking APIs](/images/guides/why-nostr-is-resilient/outage-timeline.svg)

## The Problem: Single Points of Failure

The modern internet looks decentralized on the surface. Millions of websites, apps, and services. But underneath, most of them run on a surprisingly small number of providers.

Think of it like a city's power grid. Hundreds of buildings, each with their own lights and systems. But if they all draw from the same power plant, one failure blacks out the entire city.

That's what happened in 2025. The "buildings" were different — X, LinkedIn, ChatGPT, transit apps — but they were all drawing from the same infrastructure. When that infrastructure failed, everything went dark together.

This isn't a bug. It's the architecture. Centralized platforms depend on centralized infrastructure. They have no choice.

## How Nostr Avoids This

Nostr works differently. It runs on many independent **relays** — simple servers operated by different people, in different locations, on different infrastructure.

When you publish a post on Nostr, your app sends it to multiple relays. Those relays are not owned by the same company. They don't run on the same cloud provider. They don't share a single point of failure.

If one relay goes down, the others keep working. Your posts are still accessible. Your identity still exists. The network keeps running — because it was never dependent on any single node.

<Callout type="tip">
You choose which relays to use. Most Nostr apps connect to several by default, giving you built-in redundancy without any extra setup.
</Callout>

![Centralized vs Nostr: when a single server fails everything collapses — on Nostr one relay goes down while the others keep your posts alive](/images/guides/why-nostr-is-resilient/centralized-vs-nostr.svg)

## No Company, No Kill Switch

There's a deeper point here. Every platform you use today exists at the discretion of the company that runs it.

Twitter became X because one person decided it should. LinkedIn can change its algorithm, its rules, or its terms of service overnight. If a platform decides your account violates policy — or if they simply make an error — you lose access to your network, your content, and your professional presence. There is no appeal that's guaranteed to work.

Nostr has none of these risks because there is no company behind it. Nostr is a protocol — an open standard, like email or HTTP. No one owns it. No one can change the rules for everyone. No one can flip a switch and turn it off.

Your identity on Nostr is a cryptographic key pair that you control. Not a username registered on someone else's database. It can't be suspended, revoked, or deleted by a third party.

## What This Means for You

If the platforms you depend on for professional communication went offline tomorrow — no LinkedIn, no email, no Slack — could you still reach your network?

For most people, the answer is no. And that's a risk worth thinking about.

Nostr doesn't require you to abandon the tools you use today. But it offers something none of them can: **a communication layer that no single failure can take down.** Your posts exist across multiple relays. Your identity lives on your device. Your network is resilient by design, not by promise.

The 2025 outages weren't a one-time event. They were a preview. The question is whether you're building on infrastructure that can handle the next one.

posts.title: Nostr for Beginners: Your First Steps
posts.description: A practical, step-by-step guide to getting started on Nostr. Create your identity with the Nostr WoT Extension, pick a client to explore the network, and start following people — all in about ten minutes.
posts.excerpt: Ready to try Nostr? Start by creating your identity with the Nostr WoT Extension, then pick a client to explore the network and find people to follow.
posts.seoTitle: Nostr for Beginners: Your First Steps
posts.seoDescription: A beginner's guide to Nostr. Create your identity with the WoT Extension, choose a client, connect to relays, and start following people in minutes.
posts.content:
This guide will get you up and running on Nostr in about ten minutes. No technical background required. By the end, you'll have your own identity, a client to explore the network, and people to follow.

## Step 1: Understand What You're Creating

On Nostr, your identity is a **key pair** — a public key and a private key. Think of it this way:

- **Public key (npub):** Your address. Share it freely. People use it to find and follow you.
- **Private key (nsec):** Your master password. It proves you own your identity. Never share it with anyone.

There is no email registration. No phone number. No company holding your account. You generate these keys, and they are yours permanently.

## Step 2: Create Your Identity with the Nostr WoT Extension

The best way to start on Nostr is by creating your identity with the **Nostr WoT Extension**. It acts as your key manager and signer — it keeps your private key safe in the browser and lets any Nostr client use it without ever exposing it.

<Callout type="tip">
Think of the extension as your Nostr wallet. Clients are just windows into the network — your identity lives in the extension, not in any single app.
</Callout>

1. Install the extension from the [Chrome Web Store](https://chromewebstore.google.com/detail/nostr-wot-extension/nfhdbhnnbibgkfipjbmdgcbhhjdpajjl) or [Firefox Add-ons](https://addons.mozilla.org/en-US/firefox/addon/nostr-wot/).
2. Click the extension icon and follow the setup wizard.
3. Choose **Create New Keys** — the extension generates a 12-word seed phrase for you.
4. Write down your 12 words and store them somewhere safe. This is your master backup.
5. Set a local password to encrypt your keys in the browser.
6. Fill in your profile: display name, bio, and avatar.

<Callout type="warning">
Your 12-word seed phrase is the only way to recover your account. If you lose it, there is no "forgot password" option. Store it offline — a piece of paper in a safe place or a password manager works fine.
</Callout>

For a detailed walkthrough of the extension setup, see the [Getting Started guide](/guides/getting-started).

## Step 3: Pick a Client to Explore the Network

With your identity created in the extension, you're ready to explore Nostr. A "client" is an app that lets you read posts, follow people, and publish content. Your identity works across all of them — switching clients never means losing your account.

Here are solid options:

**On desktop (web browser):**
- [Coracle](https://coracle.social) — Lightweight, focused on simplicity. Great starting point.
- [Primal](https://primal.net) — Polished interface, works in any browser.

**On mobile:**
- [Damus](https://damus.io) — iOS only. One of the most popular Nostr apps.
- [Amethyst](https://github.com/vitorpamplona/amethyst) — Android only. Full-featured.
- [Primal](https://primal.net/downloads) — Available for iOS and Android.

When you open any of these clients, they will ask for your Nostr identity. Choose "Login with extension" (NIP-07) — the client will connect to the Nostr WoT Extension automatically.

![Primal, Coracle, Damus, and Amethyst — four solid Nostr clients to start with across web, iOS, and Android](/images/guides/nostr-for-beginners/client-comparison.svg)

## Step 4: Connect to Relays

Relays are the servers that store and deliver your posts. Most clients come pre-configured with a default set, so you probably don't need to change anything right away.

If you want to check or adjust your relays:

1. Open your client's settings.
2. Look for a "Relays" section.
3. Make sure you're connected to at least 3–5 relays for good coverage.

Popular relays include `wss://relay.damus.io`, `wss://relay.primal.net`, and `wss://nos.lol`.

<Callout type="tip">
You don't need to understand relays in depth to get started. Just make sure your client is connected to a few. You can fine-tune this later as you learn more.
</Callout>

![Your client connects to multiple independent relay servers — your posts are stored across all of them simultaneously](/images/guides/nostr-for-beginners/relays-diagram.svg)

## Step 5: Follow People

Nostr is better with a feed. Here's how to find people:

- **Search by name** in your client's search bar.
- **Paste an npub** (public key) directly to find a specific person.
- **Browse trending posts** — most clients have a discover or trending section.
- **Check who others follow** — find one person you know and look at their follow list.

## Step 6: Post Something

You're set up. Say hello. The Nostr community actively welcomes newcomers.

Your post goes out to your connected relays. Anyone following you — or browsing those relays — can see it. No algorithm decides who sees your content.

## What's Next

You now have a working Nostr identity and a feed to browse. A few things worth exploring next:

- **Web of Trust:** As you follow people and they follow you back, you're building a trust network. The extension helps you visualize and manage it — useful once you have some connections. Learn more in [Understanding WoT](/guides/understanding-wot).
- **Zaps:** Nostr supports instant Bitcoin micropayments called "zaps." You can tip posts you like. This requires a Lightning wallet — see the [Setting Up a Wallet](/guides/setting-up-wallet) guide.
- **Multiple clients:** Try different apps. Your identity works across all of them. Find the one that fits how you use social media.

The most important thing: Nostr is a protocol, not a platform. There's no algorithm to game, no company to please, and no single point of failure. Your presence here is yours to build — and yours to keep.

posts.title: Getting Started with Nostr WoT
posts.description: Install the Nostr WoT browser extension on Chrome, Brave, Edge, Opera, or Firefox. Set up your Nostr identity and start building your Web of Trust in minutes.
posts.excerpt: Everything you need to install the Nostr WoT Extension and get your first trust network up and running.
posts.seoTitle: Getting Started with Nostr WoT — Extension Guide
posts.seoDescription: Install the Nostr WoT browser extension on Chrome, Brave, Edge, Opera, or Firefox. Set up your Nostr identity and start building your Web of Trust in minutes.
posts.content:
This guide walks you through installing the Nostr WoT Extension, setting up your identity, and getting your trust network running. The whole process takes about two minutes.

## Installing the Extension

The extension supports all major Chromium-based browsers and Firefox. Pick your browser and follow the steps below.

### Chrome, Brave, Edge, and Opera

1. Visit the [download page](/download) and click the link for the Chrome Web Store.
2. Click **Add to Chrome** (or the equivalent button in your browser).
3. Confirm the installation when prompted.
4. Pin the extension to your toolbar for easy access — click the puzzle piece icon in your browser and toggle the pin next to Nostr WoT.

### Firefox

1. Visit the [download page](/download) and click the link for Firefox Add-ons.
2. Click **Add to Firefox** and confirm the permissions prompt.
3. Pin the extension to your toolbar by right-clicking the icon and selecting **Pin to Toolbar**.

<Callout type="tip">
After installation, the extension icon will appear in your browser toolbar. A gray icon means you are not yet signed in. It turns purple once your identity is active.
</Callout>

## First-Time Setup

When you open the extension for the first time, the setup wizard walks you through the essentials. You will be asked to either create a new Nostr identity or import an existing one. No account registration with any third party is required.

![The first-time setup wizard offers four options: create a new identity, import an existing key, watch-only mode, or connect via Nostr Connect (NIP-46) for remote signing — keeping your private key in a separate signing app like nsecBunker or Amber](/images/guides/getting-started/setup-wizard.jpg)

## Creating a New Nostr Key

If you are new to Nostr, select **Create New Keys**. The extension generates a 12-word seed phrase (BIP39) and derives your Nostr keys from it. Your private key (nsec) and public key (npub) are both derived from this seed — you never need to handle the raw nsec directly.

1. Click **Create New Keys** in the setup wizard.
2. The extension displays your 12-word seed phrase. Write it down on paper and store it somewhere safe — a secure physical location or a password manager. If you lose it, there is no "forgot password" option. No one can recover it for you.

![Your 12-word recovery phrase is displayed once. Write it down, download it as plain text, or save an encrypted backup before confirming.](/images/guides/getting-started/key-creation.jpg)

3. Confirm you have saved the seed phrase before proceeding.
4. Set a local password to encrypt your keys inside the extension. This password is never sent anywhere.
5. Optionally fill in your profile details — display name, bio, and avatar.

<Callout type="warning">
Your 12-word seed phrase is the master key to your Nostr identity. Anyone with access to it controls your account. Store it offline, never paste it into a website, and never share it with anyone — including the Nostr WoT team.
</Callout>

## Importing an Existing Key

Already have a Nostr identity? Select **Import Existing Key**. You can restore using your 12-word seed phrase (if you created your key with Nostr WoT) or paste your nsec directly. If you prefer to keep your private key in a remote signing app, choose **Nostr Connect (NIP-46)** from the setup wizard instead — this lets you connect a bunker like nsecBunker or Amber without exposing your key.

![The import screen accepts your nsec, ncryptsec, hex private key, or 12/24-word seed phrase — the format is detected automatically](/images/guides/getting-started/import-key.jpg)

1. Click **Import Existing Key**.
2. Enter your 12-word seed phrase or paste your nsec private key.
3. Set a local encryption password.
4. The extension fetches your profile from Nostr relays automatically.

## Verifying Your Setup

Once your identity is active, click the extension icon. You should see your profile details, your npub, and an initial trust graph that starts building from your follow list. The extension fetches your contacts from relays and begins mapping your Web of Trust.

Give it a minute or two for the initial sync. After that, your trust network updates in the background as you browse.

## Post Something

You're set up. Say hello. The Nostr community actively welcomes newcomers.

Open any Nostr client — [Primal](https://primal.net), [Snort](https://snort.social), or any other — and write your first post. Add the hashtag **#introductions** so others know you're new. Your post goes out to your connected relays. Anyone following you — or browsing those relays — can see it. No algorithm decides who sees your content.

## Zaps

Nostr supports instant Bitcoin micropayments called "zaps." You can tip posts you like directly from any Nostr client. The Nostr WoT Extension lets you set up a Lightning wallet right from the extension — no third-party app required. Check the [Lightning wallet setup guide](/guides/setting-up-wallet) whenever you're ready.

## What's Next?

Now that the extension is installed, learn how to [manage your identity](/guides/managing-identity) and understand how the [Web of Trust works](/guides/understanding-wot).

posts.title: Managing Your Identity
posts.description: Learn how the Nostr WoT Extension manages your Nostr identity with NIP-07 signer support, profile management, and essential key security practices.
posts.excerpt: Your Nostr identity is the foundation of everything in the extension. Learn to manage your keys, profile, and signer settings.
posts.seoTitle: Managing Your Identity — Nostr WoT Extension Guide
posts.seoDescription: Learn how the Nostr WoT Extension manages your Nostr identity with NIP-07 signer support, profile management, and essential key security practices.
posts.content:
Your Nostr identity is the anchor for everything else in the extension — your trust network, your wallet, your permissions. This guide covers how to manage that identity, keep your keys secure, and use the NIP-07 signer that lets websites interact with your Nostr account.

## NIP-07 Signer Support

![The extension popup shows your identity status, trust graph sync state, and quick access to permissions and trust settings](/images/guides/managing-identity/extension-main.jpg)

NIP-07 is the standard that allows Nostr web applications to request signatures from a browser extension without ever seeing your private key. When you visit a Nostr client like Primal, Snort, or Coracle, it can ask the extension to sign events on your behalf.

Here is how it works in practice:

1. A website calls `window.nostr.getPublicKey()` to identify you.
2. When you post a note or update your profile, the site sends the unsigned event to the extension.
3. You see a permission prompt. If you approve, the extension signs it with your private key and sends back the signature.
4. Your private key never leaves the extension.

<Callout type="tip">
If you previously used another NIP-07 extension like nos2x or Alby, disable it before using Nostr WoT. Only one NIP-07 signer should be active at a time to avoid conflicts.
</Callout>

## Managing Your Nostr Profile

Your profile details — display name, bio, avatar, Lightning Address, and NIP-05 identifier — are stored on Nostr relays as a kind 0 event. The extension lets you view and edit these details directly.

### Viewing Your Profile

Click the extension icon and navigate to the **Identity** tab. You will see your current profile information, your npub, and the relays your profile is published to.

![The Identity tab shows your npub, profile setup status, trust graph sync state, and quick links to manage permissions and recent activity](/images/guides/managing-identity/identity-tab.jpg)

![Your profile preview shows display name, bio, NIP-05 identifier, Lightning address, and website — exactly how other Nostr users will see you](/images/guides/managing-identity/view-profile.jpg)

### Editing Your Profile

![Review your profile before publishing — confirm display name, bio, NIP-05, Lightning address, and website, then click Confirm & Publish to sign and broadcast your kind 0 event](/images/guides/managing-identity/edit-profile.jpg)

1. Click **Edit Profile** on the Identity tab.
2. Update any fields you want to change.
3. Click **Save**. The extension signs a new kind 0 event and publishes it to your relays.

Changes propagate across Nostr clients within seconds as relays distribute the updated event.

## Key Security Best Practices

Your seed phrase and nsec (private key) are the single most important pieces of data in your Nostr identity. Anyone who has either one controls your account — they can post as you, change your profile, and access your contacts. There is no password reset, no support team to call.

### Storage

- The extension encrypts your keys locally using your encryption password. They are never stored in plain text.
- Back up your 12-word seed phrase in a password manager or write it on paper and store it somewhere safe.
- Never share your seed phrase or nsec with anyone, paste them into a website, or send them in a DM.

### Encryption Password

- Choose a strong, unique password when setting up the extension.
- The extension locks after a period of inactivity. You will need this password to unlock it.
- If you forget this password, you can restore access using your 12-word seed phrase.

![When the vault is locked, enter your encryption password to unlock — your private keys never leave the extension](/images/guides/managing-identity/lock-screen.jpg)

### Multiple Devices

If you use the extension on multiple browsers or machines, restore from your 12-word seed phrase on each one. Your identity is tied to your key pair, not to any single device.

<Callout type="tip">
Your seed phrase works independently of any single browser or device. Keep it in a safe place and you can always restore your full Nostr identity.
</Callout>

## Switching Between Identities

The extension supports multiple identities. If you manage more than one Nostr account, switch between them from the Identity tab by clicking your profile avatar. Each identity has its own trust graph, wallet, and permission settings.

## What's Next?

With your identity secured, it is time to understand how the extension uses it to build your [Web of Trust](/guides/understanding-wot). If you want to connect a wallet, jump to [Setting Up Your Wallet](/guides/setting-up-wallet).

posts.title: Removing an Account
posts.description: Learn how to remove accounts from the Nostr WoT Extension — hover the account, click the cross icon, and confirm. Seed-based accounts require typing REMOVE.
posts.excerpt: Remove an account from the extension safely. Know what gets deleted, what stays on Nostr, and how seed-based accounts get an extra confirmation step.
posts.seoTitle: Removing an Account — Nostr WoT Extension Guide
posts.seoDescription: Learn how to remove accounts from the Nostr WoT Extension — hover the account, click the cross icon, and confirm. Seed-based accounts require typing REMOVE.
posts.content:
Sooner or later you will want to clean up the list of accounts in the extension — maybe an old identity you no longer use, a test account, or one you imported by mistake. The extension lets you remove accounts from the accounts selector with a single click, and applies a stricter confirmation when removing a seed-based account that only exists inside the extension.

## Before You Remove an Account

Removing an account only affects the extension on this browser. Your Nostr identity is a key pair, and the events you have published live on relays across the network. They will stay there whether or not this browser remembers you.

What removal does:

- Deletes the encrypted copy of the keys stored locally in the extension.
- Removes the account from the accounts selector.
- Clears the local trust graph, permissions, and wallet state tied to that account.

What removal does **not** do:

- It does not delete your posts, profile, or follow list from Nostr relays.
- It does not affect the same identity on other devices or other NIP-07 signers.

<Callout type="warning">
If you generated your seed phrase inside the extension and never backed it up, removing the account is permanent. There is no recovery. Export or write down your 12-word seed before continuing.
</Callout>

![The Security screen lets you export your private key (nsec) or a password-protected encrypted backup (ncryptsec) before removing the account](/images/guides/removing-accounts/backup-security.jpg)

## Removing an Account

### 1. Open the Accounts Selector

Click the extension icon and open the accounts selector at the top of the popup. You will see every account the extension is currently holding, with the active one highlighted.

![The accounts selector lists every identity stored in the extension, with the active account highlighted at the top](/images/guides/removing-accounts/accounts-selector.jpg)

### 2. Hover the Account and Click the Cross

Move your cursor over the account you want to remove. A small cross (×) icon appears on the right side of the row. Click it.

![Hovering an account row reveals a cross icon on the right — click it to start the removal flow](/images/guides/removing-accounts/hover-remove.jpg)

### 3. Confirm the Removal

A confirmation dialog opens. For an account you imported using an existing nsec, confirming here is enough — the extension assumes you still have the key somewhere else and can restore access later.

![The simple confirmation dialog asks you to confirm you want to remove the account from this extension](/images/guides/removing-accounts/confirm-dialog.jpg)

### 4. Seed-Based Accounts: Type REMOVE

If the account was generated inside the extension from a seed phrase, the confirmation is stricter. You are asked to type the word **REMOVE** (in capitals) before the button becomes active. This extra friction is there because losing the only copy of a seed is effectively losing the identity forever.

Once you confirm, the account disappears from the selector and, if it was the active one, the extension switches to another stored account or to the onboarding screen if no accounts are left.

## Why Seed-Based Accounts Need a Stricter Confirmation

When you generate a new identity inside the extension, the 12-word seed is created locally and shown to you once. The extension never sends it anywhere. If you did not copy it down, the encrypted copy in the extension is the only thing that links you to that identity. Removing it without a backup means losing the ability to sign as that npub ever again.

Imported accounts are different. If you added them using an existing nsec or seed, you already have that key material stored elsewhere — in another signer, a password manager, or on paper. Restoring is straightforward.

## Restoring an Account You Removed

To bring back a removed account:

1. Open the accounts selector and click **Add Account**.
2. Choose **Import** and paste your nsec or 12-word seed phrase.
3. The extension rebuilds the encrypted keystore, resyncs the trust graph, and the account is ready to use again.

Your local history (permissions, auto-approve rules, wallet connections) is not restored — it was tied to the deleted keystore. Nostr data like your profile, follows, and notes re-syncs from relays automatically.

## What's Next?

Now that you know how to remove accounts safely, make sure the ones you keep are well protected. Review key security in [Managing Your Identity](/guides/managing-identity), or head back to [Getting Started](/guides/getting-started) if you are rebuilding from scratch.

posts.title: Understanding the Web of Trust
posts.description: Learn how the Nostr WoT Extension builds your trust graph, what hops and trust levels mean, and how trust scores help you decide who to trust on Nostr.
posts.excerpt: The trust graph is the core of the extension. Learn what hops are, how trust scores work, and what the visualization tells you.
posts.seoTitle: Understanding Web of Trust — Nostr WoT Extension
posts.seoDescription: Learn how the Nostr WoT Extension builds your trust graph, what hops and trust levels mean, and how trust scores help you decide who to trust on Nostr.
posts.content:
The Web of Trust is the core concept behind the extension. Instead of relying on a central authority to tell you who is trustworthy, you build a trust network from the people you already follow. This guide explains how that network works and what the extension shows you.

## What the Trust Graph Shows

When you open the extension, your trust graph is a visual map of your social connections on Nostr. At the center is you. Radiating outward are the people you follow, the people they follow, and so on. Each node is a Nostr identity, and each connection is a follow relationship.

The graph answers a simple question: how connected is this person to you? Someone deeply embedded in your social network is probably more trustworthy than a brand-new account with no connections to anyone you know.

## Hops Explained

![WoT graph showing multiple hop layers — gateway nodes (gold ring) opened each new layer](/images/guides/understanding-wot/wot-hops-visual.jpg)

Hops measure the shortest path between you and another Nostr user through follow relationships.

### 1st Degree (1 Hop)

These are the people you follow directly. They are your immediate trust circle. The extension assigns the highest trust to these connections because you explicitly chose to follow them.

### 2nd Degree (2 Hops)

These are people followed by the people you follow. You may not know them personally, but someone you trust has chosen to follow them. This is a strong signal — your friends vouch for them by following them.

### 3rd Degree (3 Hops)

Three steps away from you. The connection is weaker but still present. These users are followed by someone who is followed by someone you follow. At this distance, trust is lower but still meaningful — especially if multiple paths connect you.

<Callout type="tip">
The more independent paths that connect you to someone, the higher their trust score. A person reached through five different 2nd-degree connections is more trusted than someone reached through a single 2nd-degree connection.
</Callout>

## How Trust Scores Work

Every Nostr user visible in your trust graph receives a trust score. This score is not an absolute measure of a person's character — it is a measure of how connected they are to your specific network.

The score depends on several factors:

- **Hop distance** — closer connections score higher.
- **Path count** — more paths between you and the user mean a higher score.
- **Network position** — users followed by many of your direct contacts score higher than those connected through a single chain.

The extension normalizes these factors into a score that helps you make quick decisions. When you encounter an unknown profile on a Nostr client, the extension can tell you whether that person is in your trust graph and how strong the connection is.

### Score Ranges

While the exact thresholds are configurable, a typical setup works like this:

- **High trust** — 1st-degree contacts and well-connected 2nd-degree users.
- **Medium trust** — 2nd-degree contacts with fewer paths and some 3rd-degree users with multiple connections.
- **Low trust** — 3rd-degree contacts with thin connections.
- **Unknown** — users with no path to you in the trust graph.

## Trust Is Personal

Your Web of Trust is unique to you. Two people will have completely different trust graphs because they follow different people. There is no global trust score — your graph reflects your social reality on Nostr, not some centralized reputation system. Decentralized trust means no single entity decides who is trustworthy. You and your network do.

## What's Next?

Ready to fine-tune how the extension calculates trust? Head to [Customizing Your Trust Settings](/guides/customizing-trust). Or explore the trust graph visually in the [WoT Playground](/guides/wot-playground).

posts.title: Customizing Your Trust Settings
posts.description: Adjust the trust formula, weights, and thresholds in the Nostr WoT Extension to match how you want to evaluate and score connections in your Web of Trust.
posts.excerpt: Fine-tune how the extension calculates trust. Adjust weights, thresholds, and strategies to match your preferences.
posts.seoTitle: Customizing Trust Settings — Nostr WoT Extension Guide
posts.seoDescription: Adjust the trust formula, weights, and thresholds in the Nostr WoT Extension to match how you want to evaluate and score connections in your Web of Trust.
posts.content:
The default trust settings work well for most people, but the extension gives you full control over how trust is calculated. This guide walks you through the trust formula, how to adjust weights and thresholds, and when different strategies make sense.

## The Trust Formula

At its core, the extension assigns each user a trust score based on their relationship to you in the social graph. The formula considers three primary inputs:

- **Hop distance** — how many follow-connections separate you from the user.
- **Path multiplicity** — how many independent paths connect you.
- **Path bonus** — extra trust credit for each additional path connecting you.

The result is a normalized score between 0 and 100%.

<Callout type="tip">
You do not need to understand the math to use the extension effectively. The defaults are tuned for a balanced trust network. Only adjust these if you have a specific reason.
</Callout>

## Adjusting Trust Settings

Open the extension and click the settings icon to access **Trust Settings**. You'll see two panels: a quick sensitivity slider and detailed numeric controls.

![The Trust Settings panel — Base Score per hop distance, Path Bonus per hop, and Max Path Bonus](/images/guides/customizing-trust/trust-settings.jpg)

### Trust Sensitivity Slider

The slider at the top offers a fast way to move between presets:

- **Strict** — tight trust circle, high standards. Only well-connected users score meaningfully.
- **Balanced** — the default. Standard scoring for most users. Reasonable trust with path bonus.
- **Open** — broad trust. Even distant connections carry weight.

### Base Score (%)

Controls how much base trust a user receives by hop distance, before any path bonus is applied:

| Distance | Default |
|---|---|
| 2 hops | 50% |
| 3 hops | 25% |
| 4+ hops | 10% |

Lower these values to tighten your trust circle. Raise them to be more inclusive.

### Path Bonus % (per hop)

Each additional independent path connecting you to a user adds extra trust. The bonus decreases with distance:

| Distance | Default bonus per path |
|---|---|
| 2 hops | +15% |
| 3 hops | +10% |
| 4+ hops | +5% |

A user reached through 5 different 2nd-degree contacts gets 5 × 15% = 75% path bonus on top of their base score.

### Max Path Bonus (%)

Caps the total path bonus at **50%** by default. This prevents extremely well-connected users from scoring disproportionately high regardless of how many paths connect them.

![The Trust Sensitivity modal — slider from Strict to Open, with Base Score, Path Bonus, and Max Path Bonus fields](/images/guides/customizing-trust/trust-sensitivity.jpg)

## Which Preset Should I Use?

The **Trust Sensitivity** slider maps directly to three usage profiles:

- **Strict** — you want a tight, high-signal feed. Only well-connected users score meaningfully. Good for spam filtering and focused interaction.
- **Balanced** — the default. Works well for most people. Direct follows + friends-of-friends with reasonable path bonuses.
- **Open** — you're exploring the network and want broader coverage. More noise, but more discovery.

Start with Balanced. If your feed feels too noisy, move toward Strict. If you want to discover more people, move toward Open. You can always fine-tune the individual values manually after picking a preset.

<Callout type="tip">
Changes to trust settings recalculate your entire graph. Give the extension a moment to reprocess after adjusting values. If you want to start fresh, use the **Reset to Defaults** button at the bottom of the Trust Settings panel.
</Callout>

## What's Next?

See your customized trust settings in action by exploring the [WoT Playground](/guides/wot-playground). Or continue setting up the extension by [connecting a wallet](/guides/setting-up-wallet).

posts.title: Setting Up Your Wallet
posts.description: Connect a Lightning wallet to the Nostr WoT Extension. Choose Quick Setup for instant access, NWC for your existing wallet, or LNbits for full control.
posts.excerpt: The extension supports three wallet options: Quick Setup, Nostr Wallet Connect, and LNbits. Pick the one that fits your needs.
posts.seoTitle: Setting Up Your Wallet — Nostr WoT Extension Guide
posts.seoDescription: Connect a Lightning wallet to the Nostr WoT Extension. Choose Quick Setup for instant access, NWC for your existing wallet, or LNbits for full control.
posts.content:
The extension includes a built-in Lightning wallet so you can send and receive sats without leaving your browser. This guide covers all three connection methods: Quick Setup for getting started instantly, NWC for connecting your existing wallet, and LNbits for self-hosted setups.

## Accessing Wallet Setup

1. Click the extension icon in your toolbar.
2. Navigate to the **Wallet** tab.
3. If no wallet is connected, you will see the setup screen with three options.

## Quick Setup

This is the fastest way to get a working wallet. One click and you are ready to zap.

1. Click **Quick Setup** on the wallet configuration screen.
2. The extension creates a custodial wallet linked to your Nostr identity. No registration, no forms, no extra accounts.
3. Your wallet is ready. You will see your balance (starting at zero) and options to deposit and send.

<Callout type="tip">
Quick Setup creates a custodial wallet, meaning the funds are held by the provider. This is convenient for small amounts and casual zapping. For larger amounts, consider NWC or LNbits for self-custodial control.
</Callout>

### Depositing Sats

After Quick Setup, tap **Deposit** to get a Lightning invoice or QR code. Send sats from any Lightning wallet and your balance updates within seconds.

## Nostr Wallet Connect (NWC)

If you already have a Lightning wallet that supports NWC — such as Alby, Mutiny, or any NWC-compatible provider — you can connect it to the extension.

1. In your wallet provider, generate an NWC connection string. This is usually found in the wallet's settings under "Nostr Wallet Connect" or "Connections."
2. Copy the connection string. It starts with `nostr+walletconnect://`.
3. In the extension, click **NWC** on the wallet setup screen.
4. Paste the connection string and click **Connect**.
5. The extension verifies the connection and displays your balance.

With NWC, your funds stay in your existing wallet. The extension simply sends payment requests to it. You keep full control over your sats.

Any wallet that implements the NWC protocol works, including Alby, Mutiny, and others. The protocol is open and the list of compatible wallets keeps growing.

## LNbits

For those running their own Lightning infrastructure, the extension connects directly to an LNbits instance.

1. Click **LNbits** on the wallet setup screen.
2. Enter your LNbits instance URL (e.g., `https://your-lnbits-instance.com`).
3. Enter your wallet admin key or invoice/read key depending on the level of access you want.
4. Click **Connect**.

<Callout type="tip">
Use an invoice/read key if you only want to receive and check balance. Use the admin key if you also want to send payments from the extension. Limit permissions to what you actually need.
</Callout>

## Checking Your Balance

Once connected, your wallet balance appears at the top of the Wallet tab. It updates automatically. You can also see:

- **Recent transactions** — a scrollable history of sends and receives.
- **Deposit** — generate an invoice to receive sats.
- **Send** — paste a Lightning invoice to pay it.

## Switching Wallet Providers

You can change your wallet connection at any time. Go to the Wallet tab, open **Settings**, and click **Change Provider**. This disconnects the current wallet and returns you to the setup screen. Your identity and trust settings are not affected.

## What's Next?

With your wallet connected, learn how to [zap and set up auto-approve thresholds](/guides/zapping-auto-approve). You can also [claim your Lightning Address](/guides/lightning-address) if you used Quick Setup.

posts.title: Zapping and Auto-Approve
posts.description: Learn how the Nostr WoT Extension acts as a WebLN provider for zapping, and how to configure auto-approve thresholds for frictionless micropayments.
posts.excerpt: Zap notes without switching apps. Learn how WebLN works in the extension and how auto-approve makes small zaps instant.
posts.seoTitle: Zapping and Auto-Approve — Nostr WoT Extension Guide
posts.seoDescription: Learn how the Nostr WoT Extension acts as a WebLN provider for zapping, and how to configure auto-approve thresholds for frictionless micropayments.
posts.content:
Zapping is one of the best parts of Nostr — you see a great note, you send some sats, the person gets rewarded. The extension makes this seamless by acting as a WebLN provider, and auto-approve takes it a step further by removing the confirmation step for small amounts.

## What Is WebLN?

WebLN is a standard that lets websites request Lightning payments through a browser extension. When a Nostr client wants to send a zap, it calls `window.webln.sendPayment()` and the extension handles the rest.

You do not need to configure anything for WebLN to work. Once your wallet is connected, the extension automatically registers itself as the WebLN provider for any site you visit.

## How Zapping Works

Here is the flow when you zap a note on a Nostr client:

1. You click the zap button on a note and choose an amount.
2. The Nostr client creates a Lightning invoice and sends it to the extension via WebLN.
3. The extension shows you a confirmation popup with the amount, recipient, and the note being zapped.
4. You click **Approve**, the extension pays the invoice from your connected wallet.
5. The zap event is published to Nostr, and the recipient sees it.

The entire process happens within your browser. No switching to a separate wallet app, no copying invoices.

<Callout type="tip">
If you see "No WebLN provider found" on a Nostr client, make sure the extension is unlocked and a wallet is connected. Some clients may need a page refresh to detect the provider.
</Callout>

## Setting Up Auto-Approve

For frequent zappers, confirming every 21-sat zap gets tedious. Auto-approve lets you set a threshold — any zap at or below that amount goes through instantly without a popup.

### Configuring the Threshold

1. Click the extension icon and go to the **Wallet** tab.
2. Open **Settings**.
3. Find the **Auto-Approve Threshold** field.
4. Enter your preferred amount in sats. For example, `100` means any zap of 100 sats or less is approved automatically.
5. Save. The setting takes effect immediately.

### How It Works

When a site requests a payment via WebLN:

- If the amount is at or below your threshold, the extension pays it silently. No popup, no interruption.
- If the amount is above your threshold, you see the normal confirmation popup.
- If auto-approve is set to `0`, every payment requires manual approval.

This means you can scroll through your feed, zap notes freely with small amounts, and never break your flow. Larger payments still require your explicit approval for safety.

### Per-Site Behavior

Auto-approve respects the extension's [site permissions](/guides/site-permissions). If a site does not have WebLN permission, auto-approve does not apply — the site cannot request payments at all.

## Monitoring Your Zaps

Every zap, whether auto-approved or manually confirmed, appears in your transaction history on the Wallet tab. You can review what was sent, to whom, and when.

If you notice unexpected auto-approved payments, lower your threshold or revoke the site's WebLN permission.

## Recommended Thresholds

- **21 sats** — the classic Nostr micro-zap. Conservative and safe.
- **100 sats** — covers most casual zapping.
- **500 sats** — for power users who zap frequently.
- **0 sats** — disables auto-approve entirely.

<Callout type="tip">
Start with a lower threshold and increase it once you are comfortable with the flow. You can always change it from the wallet settings.
</Callout>

## What's Next?

If you set up your wallet with Quick Setup, you can [claim your own Lightning Address](/guides/lightning-address). To control which sites can request payments, read about [site permissions](/guides/site-permissions).

posts.title: Claiming Your Lightning Address
posts.description: Claim a Lightning Address like yourname@zaps.nostr-wot.com through the Nostr WoT Extension and start receiving zaps to a memorable, human-readable address.
posts.excerpt: Get a Lightning Address like yourname@zaps.nostr-wot.com. Claim it in seconds and add it to your Nostr profile.
posts.seoTitle: Claim Your Lightning Address — Nostr WoT Extension
posts.seoDescription: Claim a Lightning Address like yourname@zaps.nostr-wot.com through the Nostr WoT Extension and start receiving zaps to a memorable, human-readable address.
posts.content:
A Lightning Address is a human-readable identifier — like an email address — that anyone can use to send you sats. Instead of generating invoices manually, you give people your address and they zap you directly. The extension lets you claim one for free when you use Quick Setup.

## What Is a Lightning Address?

A Lightning Address looks like `yourname@zaps.nostr-wot.com`. Under the hood, it resolves to a Lightning invoice endpoint. When someone sends sats to your address, the system generates a fresh invoice and routes the payment to your wallet. The sender does not need to know anything about your wallet setup.

Think of it as a permanent, memorable way for people to pay you with Bitcoin over Lightning.

## Claiming Your Address

You need to have a wallet connected via Quick Setup to claim a Lightning Address. If you used NWC or LNbits, your address is managed by your own infrastructure.

1. Open the extension and go to the **Wallet** tab.
2. Click **Settings** or look for the **Lightning Address** section.
3. Enter the username you want. It will become `yourname@zaps.nostr-wot.com`.
4. Click **Claim**.
5. If the name is available, it is yours immediately.

<Callout type="tip">
Usernames must be 3-30 characters long and can include lowercase letters, numbers, dots, and hyphens. They are first come, first served, so claim yours early.
</Callout>

## Adding to Your Nostr Profile

After claiming your address, you want it visible on your Nostr profile so people know where to zap you.

### Automatic Method

The extension offers to add your Lightning Address to your Nostr profile automatically right after you claim it. Click **Yes, update my profile** and the extension signs and publishes an updated kind 0 event with your new address.

### Manual Method

If you prefer to do it yourself:

1. Go to the **Identity** tab in the extension.
2. Click **Edit Profile**.
3. Find the **Lightning Address** field (sometimes labeled `lud16`).
4. Enter your full address: `yourname@zaps.nostr-wot.com`.
5. Click **Save**.

Either way, Nostr clients will display a zap button on your profile that uses this address.

## Receiving Zaps

Once your Lightning Address is set and published to your profile:

- Anyone viewing your profile on Primal, Snort, Coracle, or other Nostr clients will see a zap option.
- They click zap, choose an amount, and the payment routes to your wallet.
- You see the incoming sats in your wallet balance and transaction history.

No invoices to generate, no QR codes to share. The address handles everything.

## Sharing Your Address

Beyond your Nostr profile, you can share your Lightning Address in social media bios, on your website, or in email signatures. Anyone with a Lightning wallet can send sats to `yourname@zaps.nostr-wot.com` — they do not need to be on Nostr.

## Changing Your Address

If you want a different username, open the Lightning Address settings in the extension and claim a new one. Your old address will stop working, so update your Nostr profile and any other places where you shared it.

<Callout type="tip">
Before changing your address, make sure no recurring services or integrations depend on the old one. The switch is instant and the old address is released.
</Callout>

## What's Next?

Now that people can zap you, learn how to [control which sites can access your wallet](/guides/site-permissions). Or explore the [zapping and auto-approve features](/guides/zapping-auto-approve) to streamline your outgoing zaps.

posts.title: Site Permissions
posts.description: Control which websites can access your Nostr identity, request signatures, and make Lightning payments using the Nostr WoT Extension's permission system.
posts.excerpt: The extension gives you granular control over what each website can do. Learn how per-site permissions keep your keys and sats safe.
posts.seoTitle: Managing Site Permissions — Nostr WoT Extension
posts.seoDescription: Control which websites can access your Nostr identity, request signatures, and make Lightning payments using the Nostr WoT Extension's permission system.
posts.content:
Every website that interacts with the extension needs your permission. The extension does not give blanket access to your keys or wallet — each site gets its own permission scope. This guide explains how to manage those permissions effectively.

## How Permissions Work

When a website tries to access the extension's NIP-07 signer or WebLN provider, the extension checks whether that site has permission. If it does not, you see a prompt asking you to approve or deny the request.

![Pending permission requests appear at the bottom of the popup — here nostrudel.ninja is asking to read your profile information](/images/guides/site-permissions/pending-request.jpg)

Each permission decision is stored per-site. Approving signing on Primal does not automatically grant signing on Snort. You build up a whitelist of trusted sites, one at a time.

## Permission Types

The extension manages several distinct permissions for each site.

### Read Public Key

Allows the site to see your public key (npub). This is the most basic permission — the site can identify you but cannot do anything on your behalf.

![The Request Details popup for a "Read Profile Information" request — shows the site, permission type, what it does, and four response options](/images/guides/site-permissions/request-details.jpg)

### Sign Events

Allows the site to request event signatures through NIP-07. This means the site can ask you to sign notes, profile updates, reactions, and other Nostr events. You still see a confirmation prompt for each signing request unless you enable auto-approve for that site.

![When a site requests to sign an event, you see the full request details — event kind, content preview, and four options: Always Deny, Deny, Always Allow, or Allow once](/images/guides/site-permissions/permission-request.jpg)

### Encrypt and Decrypt

Allows the site to request NIP-04 or NIP-44 encrypted direct messages. The site can ask the extension to encrypt a message to a specific recipient or decrypt an incoming message.

### WebLN Payments

Allows the site to request Lightning payments through the WebLN provider. When granted, the site can present invoices for you to pay — subject to your [auto-approve threshold](/guides/zapping-auto-approve).

<Callout type="tip">
You can grant some permissions while denying others. For example, you might let a site read your public key and request signatures, but deny it WebLN access if you do not want it to prompt payments.
</Callout>

## Managing Site Permissions

### Viewing Granted Permissions

1. Open the extension and click **Settings**.
2. Navigate to **Site Permissions** (or **Permissions**).
3. You will see a list of all sites that have been granted any permission, along with what each site can do.

![The Permissions panel lists every site that has been granted access, with a count of allowed permissions per site](/images/guides/site-permissions/permissions-list.jpg)

### Revoking Permissions

If you want to remove a site's access:

1. Find the site in the permissions list.
2. Click the site name to expand its details.
3. Toggle off individual permissions or click **Revoke All** to remove all access.

![Each site's permission detail shows granular controls — Allow, Deny, or Ask per action type, plus a Revoke All button](/images/guides/site-permissions/permissions-detail.jpg)
4. The change takes effect immediately. The site will need to request permission again on its next attempt.

### Temporary vs. Permanent Permissions

When you see a permission prompt, you typically have two options:

- **Allow once** — grants the permission for this session only. When you close the tab or restart the browser, the site will need to ask again.
- **Always allow** — permanently grants the permission for this site. It persists across sessions.

Use "Allow once" for sites you do not visit regularly. Use "Always allow" for your daily-use Nostr clients.

## Best Practices for Security

Only grant the permissions a site actually needs. A Nostr client needs signing access, but a read-only explorer only needs your public key. Review your permissions list periodically and revoke access for sites you no longer use.

If a site you have never heard of requests signing or payment permissions, deny it and investigate first. Legitimate Nostr clients are well-known.

<Callout type="tip">
If a site behaves unexpectedly after granting permissions — like triggering payments you did not initiate — immediately revoke all its permissions and lower your auto-approve threshold.
</Callout>

### Secure Connections Only

The extension only exposes the NIP-07 and WebLN APIs on secure (HTTPS) connections. This prevents man-in-the-middle attacks from intercepting communication between the site and the extension.

## What's Next?

With your permissions locked down, explore the [WoT Playground](/guides/wot-playground) to visualize your trust network. Or revisit [managing your identity](/guides/managing-identity) to review your key security practices.

posts.title: The WoT Playground
posts.description: Explore the Nostr WoT Playground to visualize your trust graph, filter connections by hop distance, and see how your Web of Trust connects you to the network.
posts.excerpt: The WoT Playground lets you explore your trust graph visually. Filter by hops, search for users, and understand your network at a glance.
posts.seoTitle: The WoT Playground — Nostr WoT Extension Guide
posts.seoDescription: Explore the Nostr WoT Playground to visualize your trust graph, filter connections by hop distance, and see how your Web of Trust connects you to the network.
posts.content:
The WoT Playground is an interactive visualization of your entire trust network. It takes the abstract concept of a Web of Trust and makes it tangible — you can see exactly how you connect to any user on Nostr, explore clusters of trust, and discover patterns in your social graph.

## Accessing the Playground

![WoT Playground initial state — click the root node to start exploring](/images/guides/wot-playground/playground-initial.jpg)

There are two ways to open the Playground:

1. **From the extension** — click the extension icon and select **Playground** from the navigation menu.
2. **From the website** — visit the [WoT Playground](/playground) page directly.

The Playground loads your trust graph data from the extension. If you are not signed in or the extension is locked, you will be prompted to unlock it first.

## The Trust Graph Visualization

The main view is an interactive graph where nodes represent Nostr users and edges represent follow relationships.

### Reading the Graph

![Trust graph with hop-1 follows expanded — color-coded by trust score](/images/guides/wot-playground/playground-graph-expanded.jpg)

- **Your node** sits at the center, highlighted distinctly.
- **1st-degree connections** (direct follows) appear closest to you, typically in a bright color.
- **2nd-degree connections** form the next ring, in a slightly muted tone.
- **3rd-degree connections** are at the outer edges, more faded.
- **Edge lines** connect nodes to show follow relationships. Thicker or brighter lines may indicate stronger trust scores.

<Callout type="tip">
Click and drag to pan around the graph. Use your scroll wheel or pinch gesture to zoom in and out. Click on any node to see details about that user.
</Callout>

### Node Details

![Node detail panel showing trust score, hop distance, and trust path](/images/guides/wot-playground/playground-node-detail-v2.jpg)

Click on any node to open a detail panel showing:

- Profile information (name, avatar, npub).
- Trust score relative to you.
- Hop distance.
- Number of paths connecting you.

Click **View Profile** to open a full profile preview:

![Profile modal showing avatar, bio, trust path and recent notes](/images/guides/wot-playground/playground-profile-modal.jpg)

- Profile information (name, avatar, npub).
- Trust score relative to you.
- Hop distance.
- Number of paths connecting you.
- Mutual follows — people you both follow.

This is useful for understanding why someone has a particular trust score and how they connect to your network.

## Filtering and Searching

The Playground includes tools to narrow your view and find specific information.

### Filtering by Hops

![Filters sidebar with multiple layers expanded and gateway nodes visible](/images/guides/wot-playground/playground-filters.jpg)

Use the hop filter to show only connections at a specific distance:

- **1 hop** — shows only your direct follows. Useful for reviewing your inner circle.
- **2 hops** — adds friends-of-friends. This is where most of your day-to-day interactions happen.
- **3 hops** — the full graph. Shows the outer boundary of your trust network.

Toggle these filters using the controls in the sidebar or toolbar.

### List View

![List view showing all nodes sorted by trust score with distance and paths](/images/guides/wot-playground/playground-list-view.jpg)

Switch to **List** view to see all nodes in a sortable table. You can sort by trust score, distance, or name — useful for scanning your full trust network at a glance.

### Searching for Users

Type a name, npub, or NIP-05 identifier into the search bar. If the user is in your trust graph, the visualization highlights their node and shows the path between you.

If they are not in your graph, the search tells you so — which itself is useful information. An unknown user means they have no connection to your network within the configured hop distance.

## Analyzing Your Network

Beyond browsing, the Playground helps you understand the structure of your trust network.

### Expanding Layers

![Expanded node (gold ring) with its hop-2 follows orbiting around it](/images/guides/wot-playground/playground-gateway-node.jpg)

When you expand a node, it gets a **gold ring** to mark it as a gateway — the entry point into a new layer of your network. Its follows appear as a new orbit around it. You can expand multiple nodes to explore different branches of your Web of Trust.

### Cluster Detection

The visualization often reveals clusters — groups of densely connected users. These typically correspond to communities. You might see a cluster of Bitcoin developers, another of artists, and another of local friends. The visual grouping helps you understand the different social circles that make up your Web of Trust.

### Bridge Users

Some nodes connect otherwise separate clusters. These bridge users span different communities in your network and often have high trust scores due to multiple independent paths. If your graph is small or sparse, consider following more people to strengthen your Web of Trust.

<Callout type="tip">
The Playground is a read-only visualization. Nothing you do here changes your trust settings or follow list. Explore freely.
</Callout>

## What's Next?

Now that you have explored your trust graph, you might want to [customize your trust settings](/guides/customizing-trust) based on what you discovered. Or head back to the [getting started guide](/guides/getting-started) to review the fundamentals.

posts.title: ¿Qué es Nostr?
posts.description: Una introducción en lenguaje sencillo a Nostr — el protocolo abierto que te permite ser dueño de tu identidad y comunicarte sin depender de ninguna empresa o servidor.
posts.excerpt: Nostr es un protocolo simple y abierto para comunicación descentralizada. Ninguna empresa lo controla. Ningún servidor lo domina. Así funciona.
posts.seoTitle: ¿Qué es Nostr? Guía para Principiantes sobre el Protocolo Abierto
posts.seoDescription: Aprende qué es Nostr, cómo funciona y por qué importa. Una introducción en lenguaje sencillo para profesionales que quieren entender la comunicación descentralizada.
posts.content:
Nostr significa **Notes and Other Stuff Transmitted by Relays** (Notas y Otras Cosas Transmitidas por Relays). Es un protocolo abierto — un conjunto de reglas — que permite a las personas publicar mensajes, crear perfiles y comunicarse sin depender de ninguna empresa.

Ninguna corporación opera Nostr. Nadie puede apagarlo. Y nadie puede quitarte tu cuenta.

![Cuando publicas en Nostr, tu nota se transmite a múltiples servidores relay independientes simultáneamente — ningún servidor es dueño de tu contenido](/images/guides/what-is-nostr/broadcast-diagram.svg)

## Cómo funciona

Todas las plataformas sociales que has usado — LinkedIn, X, Instagram — funcionan de la misma manera: creas una cuenta en sus servidores, y ellos controlan todo. Tus publicaciones, tus seguidores, tus datos, tu acceso. Si la empresa cierra, cambia sus reglas o decide banearte, no puedes hacer nada.

Nostr funciona diferente. Tiene dos conceptos fundamentales: **claves** y **relays**.

### Claves: Tu identidad

Cuando te unes a Nostr, obtienes un par de claves criptográficas — piensa en ellas como un candado y una llave.

- Tu **clave pública** es tu identidad. Es como un nombre de usuario que te pertenece permanentemente. Cualquiera puede verla.
- Tu **clave privada** es tu contraseña. Demuestra que eres quien dices ser. Solo tú deberías tenerla.

Estas claves no se almacenan en el servidor de nadie. Se generan en tu dispositivo. Ninguna empresa las emite, y ninguna empresa puede revocarlas. Tu identidad es tuya.

<Callout type="tip">
Tu clave pública empieza con `npub` y tu clave privada empieza con `nsec`. Trata tu clave privada como una contraseña bancaria — nunca la compartas, nunca la pegues en un sitio web.
</Callout>

### Relays: La red

Los relays son servidores simples que transmiten mensajes. Cuando publicas algo, tu aplicación lo envía a uno o más relays. Cuando alguien quiere leer tus publicaciones, su aplicación las obtiene de esos relays.

Aquí está la diferencia clave: **no estás atado a ningún relay.** Puedes usar uno, cinco o cincuenta. Si un relay se cae, tus otros relays aún tienen tus datos. Si un relay empieza a censurar contenido, cambias a otro. Ningún relay es "el servidor de Nostr" — todos son intercambiables.

Piénsalo como el email. Tu correo no está atrapado en Gmail. Puedes usar cualquier proveedor, y todos se comunican entre sí. Nostr funciona con un principio similar, pero más simple y sin necesidad de registrarte en ningún lado.

![Tú eliges qué relays usar y puedes cambiar en cualquier momento — si uno se cae, los demás aún tienen tus publicaciones](/images/guides/what-is-nostr/relay-nodes.svg)

## Por qué importa

El internet tradicional está construido sobre la concentración. Un puñado de proveedores de nube (AWS, Azure, Cloudflare) alojan la mayoría de los servicios que usas a diario. Cuando uno de ellos falla, grandes porciones del internet se apagan — como el mundo vio repetidamente a finales de 2025.

Nostr elimina ese punto único de falla. No hay un servidor central que se caiga, no hay políticas corporativas que cambien de la noche a la mañana, y no hay cuentas que puedan suspenderse.

Para profesionales, esto significa:

- **Tu contenido es portable.** Cambia de aplicación cuando quieras — tus publicaciones y seguidores van contigo.
- **Tu identidad es permanente.** Ninguna plataforma puede borrar tu perfil o bloquearte el acceso.
- **Tu red es resiliente.** Funciona incluso cuando la infraestructura principal falla.

Nostr no es una empresa ni un producto. Es un protocolo — como el email o la web misma. Cualquiera puede construir sobre él. Y eso es exactamente lo que lo hace duradero.

posts.title: Por Qué Nostr Es Resiliente (Y el Internet No)
posts.description: Las caídas de la nube en 2025 tumbaron X, ChatGPT, LinkedIn y el transporte público. La arquitectura de Nostr está diseñada para sobrevivir exactamente este tipo de fallas.
posts.excerpt: AWS, Azure y Cloudflare fallaron a finales de 2025 — y se llevaron media internet consigo. Así es por qué Nostr no tiene ese problema.
posts.seoTitle: Por Qué Nostr Es Resiliente — Y el Internet No
posts.seoDescription: Las caídas de la nube en 2025 revelaron lo frágil que es realmente el internet. Aprende cómo la arquitectura de relays de Nostr evita los puntos únicos de falla por diseño.
posts.content:
A finales de 2025, tres de los mayores proveedores de infraestructura en la nube — AWS, Azure y Cloudflare — experimentaron caídas importantes. No al mismo tiempo. Pero lo suficientemente cerca como para dejar un mensaje claro.

Cuando AWS se cayó, no solo afectó a Amazon. Tumbó servicios de streaming, aplicaciones bancarias y herramientas empresariales de las que dependen millones de personas a diario. Cuando Azure falló, los propios productos de Microsoft se apagaron — y también los negocios construidos sobre ellos. Cuando Cloudflare tuvo problemas, sitios web y APIs alrededor del mundo quedaron inaccesibles.

X se cayó. ChatGPT se cayó. LinkedIn se cayó. Sistemas de transporte público en algunas ciudades dejaron de mostrar información en tiempo real. No por un ciberataque. Porque un solo proveedor tuvo un mal día.

![Cuando un gran proveedor de nube falla, todos los servicios construidos sobre él caen simultáneamente — redes sociales, herramientas de IA, software empresarial, APIs bancarias](/images/guides/why-nostr-is-resilient/outage-timeline.svg)

## El problema: Puntos únicos de falla

El internet moderno parece descentralizado en la superficie. Millones de sitios web, aplicaciones y servicios. Pero por debajo, la mayoría funciona sobre un número sorprendentemente pequeño de proveedores.

Piénsalo como la red eléctrica de una ciudad. Cientos de edificios, cada uno con sus propias luces y sistemas. Pero si todos dependen de la misma planta eléctrica, una falla deja a oscuras a toda la ciudad.

Eso es lo que pasó en 2025. Los "edificios" eran diferentes — X, LinkedIn, ChatGPT, apps de transporte — pero todos dependían de la misma infraestructura. Cuando esa infraestructura falló, todo se apagó junto.

Esto no es un bug. Es la arquitectura. Las plataformas centralizadas dependen de infraestructura centralizada. No tienen alternativa.

## Cómo Nostr evita esto

Nostr funciona diferente. Opera sobre muchos **relays** independientes — servidores simples operados por diferentes personas, en diferentes ubicaciones, sobre diferente infraestructura.

Cuando publicas algo en Nostr, tu aplicación lo envía a múltiples relays. Esos relays no son propiedad de la misma empresa. No corren sobre el mismo proveedor de nube. No comparten un punto único de falla.

Si un relay se cae, los demás siguen funcionando. Tus publicaciones siguen accesibles. Tu identidad sigue existiendo. La red sigue operando — porque nunca dependió de un solo nodo.

<Callout type="tip">
Tú eliges qué relays usar. La mayoría de las aplicaciones Nostr se conectan a varios por defecto, dándote redundancia incorporada sin configuración adicional.
</Callout>

![Centralizado vs Nostr: cuando un solo servidor falla todo colapsa — en Nostr un relay se cae mientras los demás mantienen tus publicaciones vivas](/images/guides/why-nostr-is-resilient/centralized-vs-nostr.svg)

## Sin empresa, sin botón de apagado

Hay un punto más profundo aquí. Cada plataforma que usas hoy existe a discreción de la empresa que la opera.

Twitter se convirtió en X porque una persona decidió que así fuera. LinkedIn puede cambiar su algoritmo, sus reglas o sus términos de servicio de la noche a la mañana. Si una plataforma decide que tu cuenta viola sus políticas — o si simplemente cometen un error — pierdes acceso a tu red, tu contenido y tu presencia profesional. No hay apelación garantizada.

Nostr no tiene ninguno de estos riesgos porque no hay ninguna empresa detrás. Nostr es un protocolo — un estándar abierto, como el email o HTTP. Nadie lo posee. Nadie puede cambiar las reglas para todos. Nadie puede apretar un botón y apagarlo.

Tu identidad en Nostr es un par de claves criptográficas que tú controlas. No un nombre de usuario registrado en la base de datos de alguien más. No puede ser suspendida, revocada o eliminada por un tercero.

## Qué significa esto para ti

Si las plataformas de las que dependes para comunicación profesional se cayeran mañana — sin LinkedIn, sin email, sin Slack — ¿podrías seguir contactando a tu red?

Para la mayoría de las personas, la respuesta es no. Y ese es un riesgo que vale la pena considerar.

Nostr no te pide que abandones las herramientas que usas hoy. Pero ofrece algo que ninguna de ellas puede: **una capa de comunicación que ninguna falla individual puede tumbar.** Tus publicaciones existen en múltiples relays. Tu identidad vive en tu dispositivo. Tu red es resiliente por diseño, no por promesa.

Las caídas de 2025 no fueron un evento aislado. Fueron un adelanto. La pregunta es si estás construyendo sobre infraestructura que pueda manejar la próxima.

posts.title: Nostr para Principiantes: Tus Primeros Pasos
posts.description: Una guía práctica, paso a paso, para empezar en Nostr. Crea tu identidad con la Extensión Nostr WoT, elige un cliente para explorar la red y empieza a seguir personas — todo en unos diez minutos.
posts.excerpt: ¿Listo para probar Nostr? Empieza creando tu identidad con la Extensión Nostr WoT, luego elige un cliente para explorar la red y encontrar personas a quienes seguir.
posts.seoTitle: Nostr para Principiantes: Tus Primeros Pasos
posts.seoDescription: Una guía práctica para empezar en Nostr. Crea tu identidad con la Extensión Nostr WoT, elige un cliente para explorar y sigue personas — sin conocimientos técnicos.
posts.content:
Esta guía te dejará funcionando en Nostr en unos diez minutos. No necesitas conocimientos técnicos. Al final, tendrás tu propia identidad, un cliente para explorar la red y personas a quienes seguir.

## Paso 1: Entiende lo que estás creando

En Nostr, tu identidad es un **par de claves** — una clave pública y una clave privada. Piénsalo así:

- **Clave pública (npub):** Tu dirección. Compártela libremente. La gente la usa para encontrarte y seguirte.
- **Clave privada (nsec):** Tu contraseña maestra. Demuestra que eres el dueño de tu identidad. Nunca la compartas con nadie.

No hay registro con email. Ni número de teléfono. Ninguna empresa controla tu cuenta. Tú generas estas claves y son tuyas permanentemente.

## Paso 2: Crea tu identidad con la Extensión Nostr WoT

La mejor forma de empezar en Nostr es creando tu identidad con la **Extensión Nostr WoT**. Funciona como tu gestor de claves y firmante — mantiene tu clave privada segura en el navegador y permite que cualquier cliente Nostr la use sin exponerla jamás.

<Callout type="tip">
Piensa en la extensión como tu billetera Nostr. Los clientes son solo ventanas a la red — tu identidad vive en la extensión, no en ninguna aplicación individual.
</Callout>

1. Instala la extensión desde la [Chrome Web Store](https://chromewebstore.google.com/detail/nostr-wot-extension/nfhdbhnnbibgkfipjbmdgcbhhjdpajjl) o [Firefox Add-ons](https://addons.mozilla.org/en-US/firefox/addon/nostr-wot/).
2. Haz clic en el ícono de la extensión y sigue el asistente de configuración.
3. Elige **Crear Nuevas Claves** — la extensión genera una frase semilla de 12 palabras para ti.
4. Anota tus 12 palabras y guárdalas en un lugar seguro. Este es tu respaldo maestro.
5. Establece una contraseña local para cifrar tus claves en el navegador.
6. Completa tu perfil: nombre visible, biografía y avatar.

<Callout type="warning">
Tu frase semilla de 12 palabras es la única forma de recuperar tu cuenta. Si la pierdes, no hay opción de "olvidé mi contraseña". Guárdala offline — un papel en un lugar seguro o un gestor de contraseñas funciona bien.
</Callout>

Para un recorrido detallado de la configuración de la extensión, consulta la guía de [Primeros Pasos](/guides/getting-started).

## Paso 3: Elige un cliente para explorar la red

Con tu identidad creada en la extensión, estás listo para explorar Nostr. Un "cliente" es una aplicación que te permite leer publicaciones, seguir personas y publicar contenido. Tu identidad funciona en todos ellos — cambiar de cliente nunca significa perder tu cuenta.

Estas son buenas opciones:

**En escritorio (navegador web):**
- [Coracle](https://coracle.social) — Ligero, enfocado en la simplicidad. Gran punto de partida.
- [Primal](https://primal.net) — Interfaz pulida, funciona en cualquier navegador.

**En móvil:**
- [Damus](https://damus.io) — Solo iOS. Una de las apps de Nostr más populares.
- [Amethyst](https://github.com/vitorpamplona/amethyst) — Solo Android. Completa en funciones.
- [Primal](https://primal.net/downloads) — Disponible para iOS y Android.

Cuando abras cualquiera de estos clientes, te pedirán tu identidad Nostr. Elige "Iniciar sesión con extensión" (NIP-07) — el cliente se conectará a la Extensión Nostr WoT automáticamente.

![Primal, Coracle, Damus y Amethyst — cuatro clientes sólidos de Nostr para empezar en web, iOS y Android](/images/guides/nostr-for-beginners/client-comparison.svg)

## Paso 4: Conéctate a relays

Los relays son los servidores que almacenan y entregan tus publicaciones. La mayoría de los clientes vienen preconfigurados con un conjunto predeterminado, así que probablemente no necesites cambiar nada de inmediato.

Si quieres revisar o ajustar tus relays:

1. Abre la configuración de tu cliente.
2. Busca la sección "Relays".
3. Asegúrate de estar conectado a al menos 3–5 relays para buena cobertura.

Relays populares incluyen `wss://relay.damus.io`, `wss://relay.primal.net` y `wss://nos.lol`.

<Callout type="tip">
No necesitas entender los relays en profundidad para empezar. Solo asegúrate de que tu cliente esté conectado a algunos. Puedes ajustar esto después a medida que aprendas más.
</Callout>

![Tu cliente se conecta a múltiples servidores relay independientes — tus publicaciones se almacenan en todos ellos simultáneamente](/images/guides/nostr-for-beginners/relays-diagram.svg)

## Paso 5: Sigue personas

Nostr es mejor con un feed. Así puedes encontrar personas:

- **Busca por nombre** en la barra de búsqueda de tu cliente.
- **Pega un npub** (clave pública) directamente para encontrar a una persona específica.
- **Explora publicaciones en tendencia** — la mayoría de los clientes tienen una sección de descubrimiento o tendencias.
- **Revisa a quién siguen otros** — encuentra a una persona que conozcas y mira su lista de seguidos.

## Paso 6: Publica algo

Ya estás configurado. Di hola. La comunidad Nostr da la bienvenida activamente a los recién llegados.
